dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to Breathing Problems at 900 E Broad St, Apt # 315, [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT for Central Rescue. What made you call 911 today?
I've had trouble breathing for the last three days. It's getting much worse now.
Could you tell me your name and what day it is today?
James… Thursday… I think.
Can you tell me the name of this place?
This is my apartment. I live on the third floor.
Your airway is open. Do you feel like anything is blocking your breathing?
No, but it feels heavy. Like I can't get enough air out properly.
I notice you have a prolonged expiratory phase. That means your breaths take longer to push air out.
How is your breathing right now-fast, shallow, or deep?
It's deep, but I hear wheezing in both lungs.
There's mild retraction, and you appear to be breathing with more effort. Any chest asymmetry?
Yes, mild retraction is visible. The effort of breathing is definitely increased.
Pulse ox is 93% on room air. That's below normal, so we need to address this.
Pulse is 95, blood pressure 119 over 75. Your heartbeat is strong and regular.
Initial pulse is 95 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is at 20 per minute, which is on the higher side.
Blood pressure is 119/75, and you feel warm.
Blood sugar is 100 currently. No signs of low blood sugar.
Oxygen saturation is 93, so you'll benefit from extra oxygen.
On a scale of 1 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate your difficulty breathing?
I am at 10 right now-I can't get comfortable with each breath.
Let's talk more about your symptoms. Have you had swelling or chest pressure?
No swelling, no chest pressure, just breathlessness and a harsh, non-productive cough.
Tell us about your medical history. You mentioned COPD and bee venom allergies earlier. Is that correct?
Yes, I have COPD and had a severe allergic reaction to a bee sting once.
What medications are you currently taking at home?
I take Albuterol regularly, but my home nebulizer has been broken so I haven't been getting treatment.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had soup and tea about four hours ago.
What happened before you called 911?
I was in here smoking and realized I couldn't take a full breath. The room is full of smoke.
I observe venous stasis on your lower legs but no redness or infection noted.
No skin rashes or evidence of active infection.
Pupils are equal and reactive-alert and oriented.
No nasal or oral secretions. Are you coughing up any mucus?
Your lungs are wheezing-more so on the left with a pronounced pleural rub.
Rhonchi are present in both lungs, with diminished breath sounds on the right side.
We'll give you a Duoneb treatment-an albuterol and Atrovent combination-via nebulizer.
We're also providing oxygen at 6 liters alongside the treatment.
An IV is being placed in your left hand to administer medication.
Based on your condition, we're following the standard COPD exacerbation protocol.
A 4-lead ECG is now being placed to monitor your heart. You're in sinus rhythm.
Pulse has dropped slightly to 94, and your blood pressure is 114 over 91 now.
SpO2 has improved to 98, and you're breathing more comfortably now.
The Duoneb reservoir flew off, so we're starting a second nebulizer to complete the full dose.
I feel better already. I can speak in full sentences now.
Minutes after the second nebulizer, your breathing is much more comfortable.
We flushed your IV with saline after the Dexamethasone and are monitoring closely.
How would you rate your breathing difficulty now? Scale is still 1 to 10.
It's maybe a 3 now. I've got steady breath and am resting better.
We're en route to [COMPANY_NAME], where they'll continue your care.
You will stay in semi-Fowler's position for transport. You're fully secured.
His Medicaid was not with him upon arrival. However, he was alert and able to sign for himself.
He was moved from the stretcher directly onto the ED bed using the bedsheet method.
The patient's walker has been left by his bedside as a personal item.
Your glucose is now reading 0, but your breathing and oxygen levels remain stable.
The full dose of Duoneb along with the corticosteroid appears to be working.
During transport, you verbally confirmed breathing comfort.
We've double-checked that the IV is patent throughout the treatment.
Your skin condition is stable and continuously monitored.
The patient was handed off in stable condition with ongoing improvement noted.
Initial pain level was described as 10, now reduced to 3 with full verbalization.
The patient was lucid and signed for himself. No confusion or delirium was observed.
Since the nebulizer treatment was safely completed, we begin the final phase of movement.
Dexamethasone was flushed with saline to ensure full delivery and IV patency.
I'll relay the EMT status to ED to prepare for your arrival.
We'll leave his best medical opinion and history on the transfer form as reference.
You were moved comfortably, and we have full communication with the ED crew ahead.
No patient identifiers were left at the site. Transport is complete.
The second nebulizer allowed full medication dose, with respiratory improvement confirmed.
No complications occurred during transport. Final reassessment status was stable and improving.
The EKG remained in sinus rhythm with occasional PVC throughout transport.
We will monitor oxygen saturation until time of arrival.
Blood pressure remained within safe parameters and steady.
The patient's breathing effort was minimal and comfortable during transport.
Breathing was sustained with no signs of deterioration during the entire transport.
IV therapy and EKG monitor remained in place with no IV infiltration noted.
It was a straightforward transfer with only one incident of a spilled nebulizer dose.
No new symptoms or signs were discovered during the transfer.
Patient showed verbal comfort and was assessed as progressing well in transport.
The IV arm was placed safely on the transfer bed with no pressure harm.
All feedback confirmed comfort during breathing while in transit.
Proper O2 and EKG monitoring occurred during transport and ED arrival.
Final position at ED was safe in bed, with the patient ready for next step in care.
Patient was stable, and transport calls confirmed appropriate care en route.
The stretcher and medical equipment were safely retrieved after arrival.
No complications or changes in patient assessment were found during or after transport.
Medical team at [COMPANY_NAME] were aware of patient influx with prior communication.
No IV complications and no medication issues occurred during transport.
He was delivered without incident and with proper follow-up with ED.
Vitals remained stable throughout transport and at final handover.
All patient items, aside from the walker, were left as is for family contact.
